headings.text: Cracked Foundations: How Japan's Retrofit Law Left 340,000 Buildings Behind
The Risk Index, Recalibrated: Twenty Cities Where Insurance Has Quietly Walked Away
North of Jakarta, a City That No Longer Trusts Its Own Soil
When the Code Caught Up, the Buildings Did Not

paragraphs.text: Across the Pacific Rim, four billion people live within striking distance of a tectonic boundary. Our autumn report tracks the fault lines — geological, political, financial — where the next disaster is already being written.
A four-month review of municipal records reveals that one in seven mid-rise concrete buildings in greater Osaka still fails 1981 seismic code — and the funding shortfall is widening, not closing.
Composite scoring of population, infrastructure age and retrofit rate places Manila, Lima and Anchorage above Tokyo for the first time in our index's nine-year history.
Subsidence is pulling Pluit downward at 25 centimetres a year. A correspondent walks the seawall with residents who have stopped waiting for the relocation lottery to call.
By Sayaka Tanaka and Rodrigo Vélez · Photographs and diagrams by the Dispatch field unit · Filed from Osaka, Kyoto and Kobe

Forty-three years after Japan rewrote its seismic building code, the country still cannot say with confidence how many of its mid-rise concrete structures meet it. The Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism publishes a national retrofit rate of 87 percent. Cross-checked against municipal cadastral records in four prefectures, the Dispatch found the figure closer to 71 percent — a gap of roughly 340,000 buildings, most of them apartment blocks, schools and small commercial structures built between 1968 and 1981.

The arithmetic of the shortfall is not subtle. A diagnostic assessment for a six-storey reinforced-concrete building costs an owner between ¥800,000 and ¥1.4 million. The retrofit itself — typically the addition of steel-frame braces or carbon-fibre column wraps — averages ¥38,000 per square metre. Public subsidies cover, depending on the municipality, between a third and two thirds of the engineering cost. They do not cover the year of displaced rent, the legal fees, the holdout tenant on the second floor.

What the Dispatch reviewed — 11,400 building records, 38 municipal subsidy programmes, and interviews with 62 engineers, owners and ward officials — was less a story of regulatory failure than of regulatory loneliness. The 1981 code was world-leading. The retrofit programme attached to it, layered in 1995 and again in 2013, presumed a level of collective action that postwar Japanese property law was never designed to deliver.

The chapters that follow walk through the 1981 inflection, the 1995 reckoning at Kobe, the 2011 audit that nobody read, and the four owners — a kindergarten director in Sakai, a 91-year-old widow in Higashinada, a real-estate cooperative in Amagasaki, and a hospital trust outside Kyoto — whose decisions, taken or deferred, are now the country's seismic exposure.
One long-form story, three short field notes, and the week's risk-index movements — delivered Friday at 06:00 local time.

From the Featured Investigation · Osaka Prefecture When the Code Caught Up, the Buildings Did Not By Sayaka Tanaka and Rodrigo Vélez · Photographs and diagrams by the Dispatch field unit · Filed from Osaka, Kyoto and Kobe Forty-three years after Japan rewrote its seismic building code, the country still cannot say with confidence how many of its mid-rise concrete structures meet it. The Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism publishes a national retrofit rate of 87 percent. Cross-checked against municipal cadastral records in four prefectures, the Dispatch found the figure closer to 71 percent — a gap of roughly 340,000 buildings, most of them apartment blocks, schools and small commercial structures built between 1968 and 1981. The arithmetic of the shortfall is not subtle. A diagnostic assessment for a six-storey reinforced-concrete building costs an owner between ¥800,000 and ¥1.4 million. The retrofit itself — typically the addition of steel-frame braces or carbon-fibre column wraps — averages ¥38,000 per square metre. Public subsidies cover, depending on the municipality, between a third and two thirds of the engineering cost. They do not cover the year of displaced rent, the legal fees, the holdout tenant on the second floor. "The law assumes a building has one owner, one decision-maker, and one bank account. None of those assumptions survive contact with a forty-year-old condominium board." What the Dispatch reviewed — 11,400 building records, 38 municipal subsidy programmes, and interviews with 62 engineers, owners and ward officials — was less a story of regulatory failure than of regulatory loneliness. The 1981 code was world-leading. The retrofit programme attached to it, layered in 1995 and again in 2013, presumed a level of collective action that postwar Japanese property law was never designed to deliver. STEEL BRACE FLOOR 3 REINFORCED FOUNDATION Figure 1 · Cross-section, typical 1970s mid-rise retrofit Diagonal steel bracing (red) is added across original concrete frames; foundation slab is widened with new piles. Average installed cost per unit: ¥4.2 million. Source: Dispatch reconstruction from MLIT specifications and three contracted engineering firms in Osaka and Hyogo prefectures, 2023–2024. The chapters that follow walk through the 1981 inflection, the 1995 reckoning at Kobe, the 2011 audit that nobody read, and the four owners — a kindergarten director in Sakai, a 91-year-old widow in Higashinada, a real-estate cooperative in Amagasaki, and a hospital trust outside Kyoto — whose decisions, taken or deferred, are now the country's seismic exposure.
